figures.caption: Table 2: Conceptual comparison between classical symplectic integrators and SNO.
Figure 2: Wave equation: snapshots of displacement and velocity at first 25 time-steps.
Figure 3: Wave equation: snapshots of displacement and velocity at first 500 time-steps.
Figure 4: Wave equation: snapshots of displacement and velocity at first 3000 time-steps.
Figure 5: Wave equation: relative L2 rollout errors against the ground truth and and energy evolution.
Figure 6: Wave equation: space–time displacement field and velocity field.
Table 3: Wave equation: Average L2 error of the state [f ; g] at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions).
Figure 7: Maxwell 1D equation: Energy evolution.

Table 4: Wave equation: Average relative energy error at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.

Table 5: EM equation: Average L2 error of the state [E;B] at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.
Figure 8: Maxwell 1D equation: snapshots of electromagnetic field at first 500 time-steps

Table 6: EM equation: Average relative energy error at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.

Table 7: Schrödinger equation: Average L2 error of the state [re; im] at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.

Table 8: Schrödinger equation: Average relative energy error at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.

Table 9: Klein–Gordon equation: Average L2 error of the state [f ; g] at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.

Table 10: Klein–Gordon equation: Average relative energy error at selected rollout steps (mean ± std over 10 random initial conditions). Bold indicates the smallest error; underline indicates the second smallest.
Figure 11: Maxwell 1D equation: space–time electric field error
Figure 12: Maxwell 1D equation: space–time Magnetic field error
Figure 13: Schrödinger equation: Energy evolution at first 50 time-steps.
Figure 14: Schrödinger equation: snapshots of real part and imaginary aprt at first 50 time-steps
Figure 17: Schrödinger equation: space–time real part error
Figure 18: Schrödinger equation: space–time imaginary error

Figure 19: Nonlinear Klein–Gordon equation: Hamiltonian energy evolution. The baseline FNO exhibits rapid energy growth and eventual blow-up, whereas the Symplectic Neural Operator (SNO) maintains bounded energy evolution close to the finite-difference (FD) reference.
Figure 20: Nonlinear Klein–Gordon equation: snapshots of displacement f(x, t) (top row) and velocity g(x, t) (bottom row) at representative times.

Figure 23: Klein-Gordon equation: relative L2 rollout errors against the ground truth for displacement (left) and velocity (right). The self-adjoint SNO significantly reduces long-time error growth compared to the baseline models.
Figure 24: Nonlinear Klein–Gordon equation: space–time displacement error relative to ground truth.
Figure 25: Nonlinear Klein–Gordon equation: space–time velocity error relative to ground truth. SNO exhibits significantly improved long-time stability compared to all baseline models.
